When you're trying to just check in with family or maybe see a nice recipe someone shared, suddenly it’s all filled with things that aren't true. Because I'm not the sharpest guy with books—my schooling wasn't that high—I sometimes just get caught up in the noise, and that makes my stomach twist up a bit, you know? With this anxiety, seeing lies everywhere feels like a heavy blanket. What I’ve learned, slowly, is that you can’t fight the whole internet, that’s too big for one person like me. But you can build up your own small defenses. First off, I try to treat social media like a shop window, not the whole store. You look, but you don’t immediately believe everything shiny. If something sounds too shocking or too perfect, I just pause. I stop scrolling for a minute. Then, I think, ‘Is this real, or is this just someone trying to sell me something, or make me angry?’ I try to look for a second source, even if it’s just a reliable news page I trust, something that feels solid, like a good piece of fabric I've knitted myself. Since I’m conscientious, I try to be disciplined about this, not just reacting when I feel anxious. I also try to remember what my faith teaches about truth; that gives me a grounding point when the digital world feels so slippery and dishonest. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: It gets me a little worried, seeing all this nonsense floating around online. When you're trying to just check in with family or maybe see a nice recipe someone shared, suddenly it’s all filled with things that aren't true. Because I'm not the sharpest guy with books—my schooling wasn't that high—I sometimes just get caught up in the noise, and that makes my stomach twist up a bit, you know? With this anxiety, seeing lies everywhere feels like a heavy blanket. What I’ve learned, slowly, is that you can’t fight the whole internet, that’s too big for one person like me. But you can build up your own small defenses. First off, I try to treat social media like a shop window, not the whole store. You look, but you don’t immediately believe everything shiny. If something sounds too shocking or too perfect, I just pause. I stop scrolling for a minute. Then, I think, ‘Is this real, or is this just someone trying to sell me something, or make me angry?’ I try to look for a second source, even if it’s just a reliable news page I trust, something that feels solid, like a good piece of fabric I've knitted myself. Since I’m conscientious, I try to be disciplined about this, not just reacting when I feel anxious. I also try to remember what my faith teaches about truth; that gives me a grounding point when the digital world feels so slippery and dishonest. It’s about slowing down the rush, being careful, and not letting the negativity steal my peace, which is something I value deeply in my life here in Chicago.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is anxious, has primary school education, and is a part-time retail worker. The tone must be cautious, slightly worried, and practical, reflecting a desire for order amidst chaos (conscientiousness) while acknowledging limitations due to lower formal education. The advice must be simple, actionable, and relatable to someone who is not a tech expert but is concerned about misinformation. I focused on the 'pause' technique and cross-referencing simple sources, grounding the advice in a sense of personal responsibility and faith, which aligns with the persona's values and anxieties.
